subject: Science
chapter_title: Exploring Substances: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 1 period = 40 minutes
Total: 1 periods · 40 minutes

cognitive_progression.stage_label: Observe and sort by indicator colour response
cognitive_progression.description: Students observe and record colour changes when household substances are tested with litmus papers, building an initial empirical basis for sorting substances.

cognitive_progression.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
cognitive_progression.description: Students classify the same set of substances using red rose extract and turmeric paper, compare results across indicators, and consolidate the three-group classification.

cognitive_progression.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
cognitive_progression.description: Students investigate what happens when an acid and a base are mixed, identify observable evidence of a chemical change, and derive the product equation.

cognitive_progression.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
cognitive_progression.description: Students reason across both central operations simultaneously — using their knowledge of acid-base classification and neutralisation to analyse and explain real-life scenarios involving ant bites, soil correction, and industrial waste treatment.

periods.stage_label: Observe and sort by indicator colour response
periods.activity_description: Students test lemon juice, soap solution, baking soda solution, vinegar, tap water, and sugar solution with both blue and red litmus paper strips, record the colour changes in an observation table, and sort samples into three preliminary groups based on results.

roles.role_description: Distributes labelled sample cups and litmus strips, demonstrates safe testing technique for the first sample, and circulates to ensure accurate recording.
Work in small groups to test each sample, record colour changes, and collaboratively sort samples into three columns in their observation table.

phases.description: The teacher introduces the National Science Day science fair narrative, poses the question of how Ashwin and Keerthi might sort everyday substances, and distributes materials.
The teacher demonstrates the litmus test with lemon juice on both blue and red litmus paper, naming the colour change precisely and recording it on the board.
Groups systematically test all six samples with blue and red litmus strips, recording colour changes in the observation table and placing sample labels into preliminary groups A, B, or C.
Groups share their sorted tables; the class reaches a shared grouping and identifies which colour change signals which group, using the terms acidic, basic, and neutral for the first time.

materials: Blue and red litmus paper strips
Labelled cups with lemon juice, soap solution, baking soda solution, vinegar, tap water, sugar solution
Board or chart for class table

periods.stage_label: Observe and sort by indicator colour response
periods.activity_title: Expanding the Sample Set — Taste, Touch, and Litmus
periods.activity_description: Students test the remaining household samples (amla juice, tamarind water, lime water, washing powder solution, salt solution) with litmus paper, record observations, and connect the colour-change groups to the sensory properties described in Activity 2.2 — sour taste for acids and soapy or bitter character for bases.

roles.role_description: Poses the question of whether taste and touch can predict litmus behaviour, guides groups to formulate and test a simple hypothesis before testing each sample.
Predict the group of each new sample based on sensory clues, test with litmus paper, record results, and identify where predictions matched or did not match observations.

phases.description: Groups review the previous period's observation table and receive the five new samples; the teacher asks: can you predict which group each sample belongs to before testing?
Each group writes a prediction column for the five new samples, citing sensory reasoning.
Groups test each sample with blue and red litmus paper, record colour changes, and compare with predictions, noting matches and mismatches.

Class discussion consolidates a complete eleven-sample observation table and identifies the naturally occurring acids named in the chapter — citric, tartaric, ascorbic, and oxalic acids — linked to specific tested samples.

materials: Blue and red litmus paper strips
Labelled cups with amla juice, tamarind water, lime water, washing powder solution, salt solution

periods.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
periods.activity_title: Red Rose Extract — A Second Indicator
periods.activity_description: Students prepare a red rose petal extract by crushing petals and filtering the coloured liquid, then test the same eleven samples, recording the colour produced (red in acidic, green in basic, and no distinctive change in neutral) and comparing results with the litmus groupings from Periods 1 and 2.

roles.role_description: Guides the preparation of the extract, ensuring filtration technique is correct, and prompts groups to map each new colour result onto the three groups already established.
Crush petals, filter the extract, test samples in dropper-sized quantities, record colours, and annotate the class observation table with a new indicator column.

phases.description: The teacher introduces the idea that plants other than lichens can serve as indicators, referring to the beetroot, purple cabbage, turmeric, jamun, and red hibiscus list, and asks: how would we make a usable indicator from rose petals?
Groups crush red rose petals in a small amount of water and filter the coloured liquid; the teacher demonstrates filter-paper folding if needed.

Groups test each of the eleven samples by adding two drops of extract to a white tile or paper, record the colour obtained, and begin annotating the existing observation table with a rose-extract column.
Quick whole-class check: does the rose extract grouping match the litmus grouping? Any disagreements are noted for discussion in the next period.

materials: Mortar and pestle or small stone for crushing
Filter paper and funnel or cloth
White tiles or white paper squares
Eleven labelled sample cups from Periods 1–2

periods.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
periods.activity_title: Turmeric Paper and the Limits of an Indicator
periods.activity_description: Students prepare turmeric paper strips, test the same samples, observe that only basic substances produce a colour change (yellow to red), and reason about why turmeric paper is less informative than litmus — constructing the understanding that different indicators have different detection scopes.

roles.role_description: Facilitates preparation of turmeric strips and poses the focused question: what can turmeric paper tell us and what can it not tell us?
Prepare strips, test samples, record results, and work out why turmeric paper leaves acidic and neutral substances equally unchanged, drafting an explanation in one or two sentences.

phases.description: Groups prepare turmeric paper by soaking filter paper strips in turmeric paste or solution and allowing brief drying; the teacher circulates to ensure even soaking.
Groups test all eleven samples on turmeric strips, record colour changes, and add a turmeric-paper column to the consolidated observation table.
Groups discuss: the indicator detected only bases — what does that mean about the information each indicator gives? Each group writes one sentence explaining the limitation.
Two groups share their sentences; the class agrees on a precise statement about the scope of turmeric paper as an indicator.

periods.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
periods.activity_title: Olfactory Indicators and the Consolidated Classification
periods.activity_description: Students prepare onion-soaked strips to experience olfactory indicators, test selected samples, observe that the onion odour changes in the presence of a base, and then consolidate all four indicator results into a single final classification table that assigns each of the eleven substances definitively to acidic, basic, or neutral.

roles.role_description: Introduces the concept of olfactory indicators and guides groups to look for patterns across all four indicator columns before drawing final classifications.
Prepare and test onion strips, record observations, and complete the consolidated classification table, resolving any column disagreements by majority-indicator logic.
Each student writes a summary of the three-group classification in their own words, citing evidence from at least two indicators for each group.

phases.description: The teacher presents the concept of olfactory indicators, asks students to predict whether smell could serve as a classification tool, and distributes onion-soaked strips.
Groups test a subset of samples (at least one acid, one base, one neutral) with onion strips by wafting over the sample, recording whether the characteristic onion odour is lost or retained.
Groups lay out all four indicator columns side by side and resolve any inconsistencies to produce a consolidated classification of all eleven substances.

Each student individually writes a two-paragraph summary: paragraph one states the classification of all eleven substances with supporting evidence from indicators; paragraph two explains what makes turmeric paper less informative than litmus.
Pairs exchange summaries and give one specific comment; two summaries are read aloud to close the period.

periods.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
periods.activity_title: Acid Meets Base — Watching Neutralisation Happen
periods.activity_description: Students progressively add lemon juice to lime water containing blue litmus solution, observe the colour shift from blue to red as the base is consumed, identify this as evidence of a chemical change, and record the point at which the solution is neither acidic nor basic.

roles.role_description: Demonstrates the initial set-up — lime water with blue litmus solution in a beaker — and directs students to add lemon juice drop by drop while watching for colour change.

Add lemon juice dropwise, count drops to the colour-change point, record observations at each stage, and note whether the change is reversible by attempting to restore the original colour with lime water.

phases.description: The teacher sets up the demonstration beaker with lime water and blue litmus solution; students predict what will happen when lemon juice is added.
Each group adds lemon juice drop by drop to their beaker, recording colour at every fifth drop and noting the transition point from blue through purple to red.
Groups attempt to restore the blue colour by adding more lime water dropwise, record what happens, and discuss whether the change is reversible in the same way as, say, dissolving salt.
Class shares observations; the teacher introduces the term neutralisation reaction and writes the word equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water + Heat on the board.

materials: Small beakers or transparent plastic cups

periods.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
periods.activity_title: Products of Neutralisation — Salt, Water, and Heat
periods.activity_description: Using the observed neutralisation experiment as the anchor, the teacher develops the concept that neutralisation is a chemical change, identifies salt and water as products, explains that heat is also released, and distinguishes neutralisation from physical changes studied earlier.

roles.role_description: Explains the product equation systematically, uses the experimental colour-change observation as evidence, and highlights why product formation distinguishes this as a chemical rather than physical change.
Take structured notes using a provided scaffold — three columns: observation from Period 6, what it indicates, and the chemical concept it illustrates — and pose clarifying questions.

phases.description: Students review their Period 6 observation tables; the teacher asks: what evidence from the experiment tells us a new substance was formed?

The teacher explains the neutralisation equation step by step — acid reacts with base, salt is the product of the combined ions, water forms, and heat is released — connecting each product to observable evidence where possible.
Students complete the three-column scaffold, linking each experimental observation to the chemical concept it illustrates.
Students compare their scaffolds with a partner, then three students share one row each; the teacher clarifies any misconceptions about the difference between a chemical and physical change.

periods.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
periods.activity_title: Why Does Baking Soda Help After an Ant Bite?
periods.activity_description: Students analyse three real-world scenarios from Section 2.3 — ant bite treatment, soil correction, and industrial waste neutralisation — identifying the acidic or basic nature of each substance involved, predicting the corrective action required, and justifying their reasoning using both classification knowledge and the neutralisation equation.

roles.role_description: Presents each scenario as a problem to be solved, prompts groups to identify what chemical information they need, and asks them to reason to a solution before revealing the textbook explanation.
Read each scenario, identify the acid or base involved, apply the neutralisation concept to propose and justify a corrective action, and compare their proposal with the chapter's explanation.

phases.description: The teacher frames the session: the three scenarios are real problems that required someone to use both classification and neutralisation reasoning to solve — groups will attempt to solve them before reading the answers.

Groups work through Scenario 1 (ant bite — formic acid) and Scenario 2 (soil acidity and basicity), writing the acid or base present, the corrective substance, and the neutralisation equation for each.
Groups tackle Scenario 3 (acidic factory waste in a lake), extending the reasoning to an environmental scale and discussing whether adding a base before release is always sufficient.

Each group presents one scenario solution; the class evaluates the reasoning, not just the answer, and the teacher highlights where classification knowledge and neutralisation knowledge both had to be used together.
Students individually write one sentence for each scenario explaining the corrective action in terms of the neutralisation reaction.

materials: Scenario cards or worksheet with Situations 1–3 from Section 2.3

periods.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
periods.activity_description: Students receive a set of clues about the opening science fair mystery — a message written on turmeric-coated paper using soap solution — and reconstruct the chemistry behind it, using their knowledge of turmeric paper's response to bases and the neutralisation of the soap base by an acidic substance, to explain what made the message appear and disappear.

roles.role_description: Restates the opening mystery and provides a structured clue sheet; does not reveal the answer, but confirms or challenges student explanations using evidence-based questioning.

Work independently to reconstruct the chemistry of the mystery in writing — identifying the indicator used, the substance that wrote the message, the colour change produced, and the chemical nature of each component.

phases.description: The teacher re-reads the opening science fair narrative and asks: now that you know what you know, what was the chemistry behind the mystery message?
Students work individually to write a step-by-step chemical explanation of the mystery, naming the indicator, the substance used for writing, the colour change it caused, and why it worked.
Students pair up and compare explanations, identifying any steps where reasoning about indicator behaviour or base-detection differs.
Two or three students share their explanations; the teacher uses these to close the chapter's narrative arc, confirming that turmeric paper was used and soap solution caused the colour change.

materials: Clue sheet with the science fair mystery description

periods.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
periods.activity_title: New Substance, Same Tools — Applying Indicators to an Unknown
periods.activity_description: Each group receives one unlabelled sample and must determine whether it is acidic, basic, or neutral using whichever indicators they choose, record their reasoning, predict what would be needed to neutralise it, and present their classification and neutralisation proposal to the class.

roles.role_description: Distributes unlabelled samples (chosen from safe, chapter-tested substances), sets the task framing, and uses group presentations to assess whether students can transfer both classification and neutralisation reasoning to an unfamiliar item.
Select indicators, conduct tests, classify the unknown, propose a neutralising agent with justification, and present findings with evidence.

phases.description: The teacher distributes unlabelled sample cups and frames the task: classify the substance and propose a neutralising agent if it is acidic or basic.
Groups select and apply at least two indicators, record colour changes, classify the substance, and write a neutralisation justification citing the word equation.
Each group presents: substance group, evidence from indicators chosen, and proposed neutralising agent with reasoning based on the neutralisation reaction.
The teacher reveals the identity of each sample and the class confirms whether the classifications and neutralisation proposals were correct.

materials: Unlabelled cups containing safe samples (e.g., dilute vinegar, dilute baking soda solution, salt water)
Red rose extract (if available from Period 3)

assessment_items.stage_label: Observe and sort by indicator colour response
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can record the colour change produced by each tested substance on both blue and red litmus paper and sort the substances into three groups — one producing a colour change on blue paper, one on red paper, and one producing no change on either — with evidence from the observation table.

competency.competency_text: Classification of matter by chemical characteristics, including acids and bases.

assessment_items.question_text: A student dips a strip of blue litmus paper and a strip of red litmus paper into a cup of vinegar. Which result would the student record?

options.label: A
options.text: Blue litmus turns red; red litmus stays red.

options.label: B
options.text: Blue litmus stays blue; red litmus turns blue.

options.label: C
options.text: Both blue and red litmus papers turn purple.

options.label: D
options.text: Blue litmus stays blue; red litmus stays red.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has reversed the indicator rule — confusing the response of an acid with that of a base; this is a classic misconception about which litmus colour changes in which chemical environment.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student recalls that litmus can show purple at a transitional or neutral point but incorrectly applies this to a clearly acidic substance like vinegar, confusing the neutral transition colour with a general acidic response.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has not engaged with the indicator rule at all and believes neutral substances and acidic substances produce the same null result on both papers.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who struggle with reading colour descriptions can be asked to physically perform the test and match their strip to a colour chart. Students who have colour-vision differences may be paired with a peer who can confirm the colour verbally.

assessment_items.stage_label: Observe and sort by indicator colour response
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can record the colour change produced by each tested substance on both blue and red litmus paper and sort the substances into three groups — one producing a colour change on blue paper, one on red paper, and one producing no change on either — with evidence from the observation table.

competency.competency_text: Classification of matter by chemical characteristics, including acids and bases.

assessment_items.question_text:
During an activity, a student tests four substances with both blue and red litmus paper. The table below shows the results.

Substance | Blue litmus | Red litmus
P         | No change   | No change
Q         | Turns red   | No change
R         | No change   | Turns blue
S         | No change   | No change

Which substances belong to the same group?

options.label: A
options.text: P and S

options.label: B
options.text: Q and R

options.label: C
options.text: P and R

options.label: D
options.text: Q and S

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student is grouping by the criterion that both substances caused a colour change on one paper, ignoring that Q and R produced changes on different papers and therefore belong to opposite groups — a confusion between the two reactive groups.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has correctly identified that R is not acidic but has incorrectly grouped R with the neutral substances P and S, failing to distinguish the base group (red litmus turns blue) from the neutral group (no change on either paper).
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has selected one substance from the acid group (Q) and one from the neutral group (S), indicating a failure to use the no-change criterion on both papers as the defining feature of the neutral group.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who find the table format challenging can first physically sort cards labelled with each result before answering. The question can also be presented with colour-coded cells if the classroom display permits.

assessment_items.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can classify a set of substances as acidic, basic, or neutral by comparing results across multiple natural indicators and can explain, with reference to turmeric paper's behaviour, why different indicators provide different amounts of discriminating information.

competency.competency_text: Classification of matter by chemical characteristics, including acids and bases.

assessment_items.question_text: A student tests an unknown liquid with turmeric paper and finds no colour change. Which conclusion is best supported by this result alone?

options.label: A
options.text: The liquid is either acidic or neutral, but turmeric paper cannot tell us which.

options.label: B
options.text: The liquid is neutral because no colour change occurred on any paper.

options.label: C
options.text: The liquid is acidic because turmeric paper only changes colour for bases.

options.label: D
options.text: The liquid is basic because a strong base would not change turmeric paper.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has correctly applied the no-change outcome to reach a neutral classification but has forgotten that turmeric paper also gives no change for acidic substances, conflating turmeric paper's null result with the litmus null result for neutral substances.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has partially engaged with the chapter's claim about turmeric paper's specificity but has drawn an unjustified definitive conclusion — knowing what the indicator detects does not let us conclude the substance is acidic, only that it is not basic.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has inverted the indicator logic, reasoning that a strong base would suppress the colour change rather than cause it — a misconception about intensity of response.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who struggle with the abstract logic can be asked to refer to their completed four-indicator table from the hands-on activity and locate a substance that gave no change on turmeric but was confirmed acidic by litmus.

assessment_items.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can classify a set of substances as acidic, basic, or neutral by comparing results across multiple natural indicators and can explain, with reference to turmeric paper's behaviour, why different indicators provide different amounts of discriminating information.

competency.competency_text: Classification of matter by chemical characteristics, including acids and bases.

assessment_items.question_text: A student wants to distinguish between a neutral substance and an acidic substance using only natural indicators. Which indicator should the student choose, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Litmus paper, because it produces different colour changes for acidic and neutral substances.

options.label: B
options.text: Turmeric paper, because it turns red only with acidic substances.

options.label: C
options.text: Turmeric paper, because it gives no change for both acidic and neutral substances.

options.label: D
options.text: Red rose extract, because it turns green for neutral substances and red for acidic substances.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has confused turmeric paper's specificity — it turns red only with bases, not acids — indicating a recall error about which chemical group causes the colour change in turmeric paper.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student correctly recalls that turmeric paper gives no change for acids but has drawn the wrong operational conclusion, choosing an indicator that cannot make the distinction rather than one that can.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has invented a colour response for red rose extract with neutral substances that is not supported by the chapter — the extract gives red for acids and green for bases, not a distinctive colour for neutral — indicating a confusion between what is observed for bases and what is observed for neutral substances.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who find the reasoning abstract can be asked to physically annotate their consolidated indicator table, highlighting the cells that show different values for acidic and neutral rows in each indicator column, before answering.

assessment_items.stage_label: Classify substances as acidic, basic, or neutral using multiple indicators
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can classify a set of substances as acidic, basic, or neutral by comparing results across multiple natural indicators and can explain, with reference to turmeric paper's behaviour, why different indicators provide different amounts of discriminating information.

competency.competency_text: Classification of matter by chemical characteristics, including acids and bases.

assessment_items.question_text:
Riya tested two unknown substances — Substance X and Substance Y — using both litmus paper and turmeric paper. Her results are shown below.

Substance | Blue litmus | Red litmus | Turmeric paper
X         | Turns red   | No change  | No change
Y         | No change   | No change  | No change

(a) Classify Substance X and Substance Y as acidic, basic, or neutral. Give a reason for each classification.
(b) Riya says she could have identified Substance X using only turmeric paper. Is she correct? Explain why or why not.
expected_elements: Correct classification of Substance X as acidic, supported by the blue litmus turning red result.
Correct classification of Substance Y as neutral, supported by no colour change on any indicator.
A clear explanation that Riya is incorrect because turmeric paper gives no colour change for both acidic and neutral substances and therefore cannot distinguish between them.
A reference to which indicator (litmus) is needed to distinguish acidic from neutral substances.

SCR.inclusivity: Students who find written explanation difficult can use a two-column table to lay out evidence before writing prose. Students who need additional scaffolding can be directed to their completed four-indicator observation table to locate the row corresponding to each result pattern.

assessment_items.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can describe the observable evidence from the lemon juice–lime water experiment that signals a chemical change has occurred and can write the word equation for neutralisation, identifying salt, water, and heat as products.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text: In the lemon juice and lime water experiment, a student added lemon juice drop by drop to a beaker of lime water containing blue litmus solution. The blue colour slowly changed to red. What does this colour change indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The base in the lime water is being consumed by the acid, producing new substances.

options.label: B
options.text: The lemon juice is dissolving into the lime water without forming any new substance.

options.label: C
options.text: The litmus solution itself is reacting with the beaker to produce an acid.

options.label: D
options.text: The lime water is becoming more basic as more lemon juice is added.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has not engaged with the evidence that a colour change signals chemical change and instead interprets the mixing as a physical process of dissolution — a confusion between physical mixing and chemical reaction.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has focused on the indicator rather than the reactants, incorrectly attributing the colour change to a reaction between litmus and the glassware, indicating a misunderstanding of the role of the indicator as a detector rather than a reactant.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has reversed the directional logic of the experiment — adding an acid to a base cannot make the solution more basic; this indicates a misconception about how acid-base mixing shifts the chemical character of the solution.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who benefit from concrete anchoring can be asked to sketch the sequence of colour changes they observed in the activity before selecting an answer. Students who process language slowly can be given extra time on this question.

assessment_items.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can describe the observable evidence from the lemon juice–lime water experiment that signals a chemical change has occurred and can write the word equation for neutralisation, identifying salt, water, and heat as products.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text: Which of the following correctly identifies all three products of a neutralisation reaction?

options.label: A
options.text: Salt, water, and heat

options.label: B
options.text: Salt, carbon dioxide, and water

options.label: C
options.text: Water, an indicator, and heat

options.label: D
options.text: Salt and water only

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has confused neutralisation with a reaction that produces a gas — possibly confusing it with reactions of acids with carbonates or metals encountered in other contexts — introducing carbon dioxide as a product that does not feature in the chapter's neutralisation equation.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has conflated the role of the indicator (a detection tool used in the experiment) with the products of the reaction itself, failing to distinguish between experimental procedure and chemical outcome.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has recalled two of the three products correctly but has omitted heat, indicating incomplete recall of the word equation or a failure to treat heat release as a product category.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who find abstract product identification difficult can first draw the word equation arrow with reactants on the left and then attempt to fill in the right side from memory before selecting.

assessment_items.stage_label: Explain the neutralisation reaction as a chemical change
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can describe the observable evidence from the lemon juice–lime water experiment that signals a chemical change has occurred and can write the word equation for neutralisation, identifying salt, water, and heat as products.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text:
In an experiment, a student adds lemon juice drop by drop to lime water containing blue litmus solution and records the colour at every five drops. After 20 drops the colour is purple; after 35 drops it is fully red.

(a) Write the word equation for the reaction taking place in this experiment.
(b) Identify two pieces of observable evidence from the experiment that show a chemical change — not just a physical mixing — has occurred.
(c) The student tries to restore the original blue colour by adding more lime water. The colour shifts to purple but does not return to blue no matter how much lime water is added. What does this suggest about the reaction that has already taken place?
look_for: A correctly written word equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water + Heat, with lemon juice identified as the acid and lime water as the base.

Two distinct pieces of observable evidence for chemical change — at minimum: the irreversible colour change from blue to red, and the formation of new products (salt and water) that were not present in the original reactants; reference to heat release is also acceptable.

A reasoned explanation of part (c) that references the irreversibility of the reaction — the acid and base have already combined to form products; simply adding more base cannot undo the products already formed, though it can shift the mixture back toward neutral temporarily.

ECR.inclusivity: Students who find extended writing difficult can be offered a sentence-starter scaffold for part (c): 'Once the acid and base have reacted, they form…, which means that…'. Students who process the experiment more concretely can be asked to sketch the beaker at 0, 20, and 35 drops before writing.

assessment_items.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can identify the acidic or basic nature of a substance involved in an everyday situation, select an appropriate neutralising agent, and justify the selection using the neutralisation word equation — demonstrating simultaneous use of classification and chemical change reasoning.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text: A farmer tests soil from a field and finds it is highly acidic. Crop growth in this soil is poor. Which action should the farmer take, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Add lime to the soil, because lime is a base that will neutralise the excess acid.

options.label: B
options.text: Add more chemical fertiliser, because fertilisers make soil fertile regardless of its chemical nature.

options.label: C
options.text: Add compost to the soil, because compost releases acids that will neutralise the base.

options.label: D
options.text: Add vinegar to the soil, because vinegar is acidic and will react with the soil acid to form salt.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has not applied the classification-based reasoning required and instead relies on a general, non-chemical notion of soil fertility, failing to connect the soil's chemical nature to the need for neutralisation.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has recalled the correct chapter detail — compost releases acids — but has applied it to the wrong situation; compost is used for basic soils, not acidic soils. This indicates a confusion between the two soil-correction scenarios in Section 2.3.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has confused the neutralisation principle, believing that adding more of the same type of substance (acid on acid) can produce a salt — a misconception about what the two reactants in a neutralisation reaction must be.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who find the application context unfamiliar can be directed to re-read Situation 2 from Section 2.3 before answering. Students who benefit from visual support can annotate a simple diagram of the word equation with the specific substances in each scenario.

assessment_items.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can identify the acidic or basic nature of a substance involved in an everyday situation, select an appropriate neutralising agent, and justify the selection using the neutralisation word equation — demonstrating simultaneous use of classification and chemical change reasoning.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text: A factory releases waste water that turns blue litmus paper red. An environmental officer recommends adding a substance to the waste before it enters a lake. Which substance should be added, and what reaction will occur?

options.label: A
options.text: A basic substance should be added; it will neutralise the acid in the waste, producing salt, water, and heat.

options.label: B
options.text: An acidic substance should be added; it will react with the acid to dilute it and reduce harm.

options.label: C
options.text: A neutral substance such as water should be added; neutralisation requires a neutral reactant.

options.label: D
options.text: A basic substance should be added; the indicator in the waste will change colour but no new substances will form.

what_each_option_reveals.B: The student has not applied the neutralisation principle and instead uses a non-chemical dilution logic, confusing concentration reduction with chemical neutralisation and incorrectly proposing the same type of substance as a corrective agent.
what_each_option_reveals.C: The student has confused the term neutralisation — a chemical reaction producing a neutral product — with adding something neutral, indicating a misconception about the definition of a neutralisation reaction.
what_each_option_reveals.D: The student has correctly identified the required reagent type (base) but has not engaged with the chemical change aspect, treating the indicator colour change as the outcome rather than recognising that new products are formed.

MCQ.inclusivity: Students who need grounding in the scenario can be asked to first identify which litmus paper result tells us about the waste, then which substance type is needed to correct it, before reading the options.

assessment_items.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can identify the acidic or basic nature of a substance involved in an everyday situation, select an appropriate neutralising agent, and justify the selection using the neutralisation word equation — demonstrating simultaneous use of classification and chemical change reasoning.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.question_text:
A red ant bites Meena on her arm. Her friend suggests rubbing baking soda paste on the bite; her mother suggests rubbing lemon juice on it instead.

(a) Identify the chemical nature of the substance injected by the ant and name the specific acid involved.
(b) Evaluate the two suggestions using your knowledge of acid-base classification and neutralisation. Which suggestion is correct, and why is the other suggestion ineffective or harmful?
(c) Write the general word equation that applies to the effective treatment.
look_for: Correct identification of formic acid as acidic, with the name formic acid stated.

A reasoned evaluation that baking soda is correct because it is a base that will neutralise the formic acid, and that lemon juice is ineffective or harmful because it is also acidic and cannot neutralise an acid — acid on acid does not produce neutralisation.
The word equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water + Heat, applied to this context (formic acid + baking soda → salt + water + heat).
Language that clearly connects the classification step (identifying the nature of each substance) with the neutralisation step (predicting the outcome of combining them).

ECR.inclusivity: Students who find the evaluation format challenging can be offered a three-step prompt: step 1 — what is the nature of the ant-injected substance? Step 2 — what is the nature of each suggested remedy? Step 3 — which pairing produces a neutralisation reaction? Students who express understanding better orally can give a spoken response recorded by the teacher.

assessment_items.stage_label: Apply classification and neutralisation reasoning to unfamiliar everyday situations
assessment_items.implied_lo_assessed: The student can identify the acidic or basic nature of a substance involved in an everyday situation, select an appropriate neutralising agent, and justify the selection using the neutralisation word equation — demonstrating simultaneous use of classification and chemical change reasoning.

competency.competency_text: Chemical changes in matter, including the neutralisation reaction and its products.

assessment_items.visual_stimulus: A data table is provided to students with the following columns: Scenario description | Litmus test result on the problem substance | Nature of the problem substance (acidic/basic/neutral) | Proposed corrective substance | Nature of corrective substance | Expected products of treatment. Three rows of data are partially filled: Row 1 — A bee sting makes the skin swell and burn (bee venom is basic); litmus result: red litmus turns blue; Row 2 — Waste from a battery factory makes a stream acidic; litmus result: blue litmus turns red; Row 3 — A region's soil is tested and found to be very basic, reducing wheat yield. Litmus result: red litmus turns blue. The columns for Nature of problem substance, Proposed corrective substance, Nature of corrective substance, and Expected products are left blank for students to complete.
assessment_items.task: Three new situations are described in the table. For each situation: (1) Identify the chemical nature of the problem substance using the litmus result provided. (2) Select an appropriate corrective substance from this list — baking soda, vinegar, lime, compost — and state its chemical nature. (3) Complete the expected products column using the neutralisation word equation. (4) After completing the table, write two to three sentences explaining why choosing the wrong type of corrective substance would fail to solve the problem in any of these situations.
assessment_items.scaffold: Use the table provided. For each row: identify whether the problem substance is acidic, basic, or neutral from the litmus result → decide which type of substance is needed to neutralise it → select the correct corrective substance from the list → fill in the expected products using: Acid + Base → Salt + Water + Heat → write your explanation in the space below the table.
format_of_output: Completed three-row data table with all blank columns filled
A written explanation of two to three sentences below the table

OPEN_TASK.format_type: Data interpretation task (provided table or graph)
OPEN_TASK.format_rationale: The Final Stage implied LO requires simultaneous use of classification and neutralisation reasoning across multiple real-world contexts; a partially completed data table is the most natural demonstration vehicle because it makes both operations visible in one structured output — each row requires a classification decision followed by a neutralisation application.
OPEN_TASK.what_this_demonstrates: A complete response demonstrates that the student can read an indicator result, assign a chemical classification, select a chemically compatible corrective agent, predict reaction products, and articulate why an incorrect choice would be ineffective — integrating both central operations of the chapter.
OPEN_TASK.reading_the_scaffold: The scaffold is intentionally directional: each step maps to one cognitive operation — step 1 is classification from indicator evidence, step 2 is selection using neutralisation logic, step 3 is product knowledge application, step 4 is evaluative reasoning about failure cases. A student who completes only the table without the written explanation has demonstrated application but not evaluation. A student who writes the explanation without completing the table has not demonstrated the classification step. Both components are necessary for a full demonstration.
OPEN_TASK.strong_vs_weak_markers: Strong responses: correctly classify all three problem substances using the litmus result, select the appropriate corrective substance with the correct chemical nature stated, accurately complete the products column citing salt, water, and heat, and write an explanation that generalises the principle — e.g., an acid cannot neutralise another acid because neutralisation requires one acid and one base. Weak responses: correctly complete the table mechanically but write a vague or circular explanation, or correctly explain the principle but make classification errors in the table — indicating that one of the two central operations is not yet integrated with the other.
OPEN_TASK.inclusivity: Students who find the written explanation difficult can first articulate their reasoning orally to a partner and then write a condensed version. Students who need additional scaffolding for the table can be given one fully completed example row before beginning. Teachers may substitute a predict-and-explain task using the same three scenarios if the table format is inaccessible for a particular student.
